title: Jobless spike stalls RBA’s next rate hike, for now
full_text:
The Reserve Bank’s next interest rate hike has been pushed back for now, but don’t get too excited.
A sharp rise in Australia’s unemployment rate in April means the RBA’s monetary policy board will almost certainly decide to keep interest rates on hold when it meets in mid-June.
But without clearer signs of a sharp economic slowdown or a reduction of inflationary pressures, most economists still think the RBA will need to lift rates again by the end of the year.
NAB shifted its call for a final rate hike from June to August after the jobless rate hit a four-and-a-half-year high of 4.5 per cent, against expectations it would hold at 4.3 per cent.
Employment itself fell by 18,600 in April, undershooting expectations of a 15,000 rise.
“These data can be volatile month to month, but this is a soft print and one that challenges the RBA’s statement on monetary policy judgment of labour market resilience,” NAB senior economist Taylor Nugent says.
By the time of the August meeting, economic data should have softened enough to avoid any further rate hikes, according to ANZ head of Australian economics Adam Boyton.
He says the trend decline in the employment-to-population ratio in the past year suggests the time is approaching when the RBA will need to describe the labour market as “balanced” rather than tight.
Westpac also rules out a June hike but still expects two more rises by the end of the year.
Economist Ryan Wells says the result was “genuinely weaker than expected”, though he cautions that Easter distorted the figures. The survey period fully overlapped the long weekend this year – a rare occurrence – which tends to depress numbers and should largely reverse in May. Employment among 15-to-24-year-olds collapsed by 56,400, accounting for much of the headline fall.
Citi chief economist Josh Williamson also urges caution, noting that hours worked rose strongly, underemployment fell and average hours per employee hit a four-year high.
“If there was a business cycle-based turn in the labour market, these metrics would likely have deteriorated as well,” he says.
Not everyone sees another hike as inevitable.
HSBC chief economist Paul Bloxham says the data is “the sort of decisive marker” he has been watching for to confirm the economy has already entered a downturn. Taken together with sharp falls in consumer and business confidence, falling auction clearance rates and declining house prices, he believes the RBA is now set to remain on hold for some time. Once the jobs market is decisively loosening, he argues, the RBA can point to downward pressure on wages growth as a disinflationary force – and has in any case shown a stronger preference for protecting full employment than rushing inflation back to target.
Still, UBS chief economist George Tharenou says the labour market remains relatively tight.
He estimates the non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment has risen to around 4.75 per cent, meaning 4.5 per cent still implies the economy is running hot. UBS expects a 25 basis point rise in August, lifting the cash rate to a cycle high of 4.60 per cent.
Money markets now imply just a 6 per cent chance of a June hike, down from around 14 per cent before the data, though another rise is fully priced in by November.
The RBA has delivered three back-to-back hikes totalling 75 basis points since February.
In May it forecast that core inflation would not return to the midpoint of its 2–3 per cent target band until mid-2028, based on assumptions that included the cash rate rising to 3.7 per cent by year end from its current 3.35 per cent.
After May’s near-unanimous decision to raise rates, RBA governor Michele Bullock said policy was “a bit restrictive” and that this “gives the board space to see how the conflict plays out and the response of Australian households and businesses to the shock".
The Iran war has pushed crude oil prices up by more than 50 per cent. Capital Economics senior economist Abhijit Surya warns that underlying inflation is set to accelerate further as higher diesel costs flow through to consumer prices, and still expects a 25 basis point hike in the third quarter. “The bank's tightening cycle has further to run,” he says.
Nugent sums up the mood among most forecasters. “The balance of risks has shifted in favour of the board’s characterisation that they have some ‘space’ to monitor incoming data for both inflation and activity impacts due to higher oil prices and uncertainty coming out of the Middle East,” he says. Next week’s April consumer price index reading will be the next key test. A hotter-than-expected print could quickly revive talk of a June move – however unlikely that now appears.
author: DAVID ROGERS, MARKETS
heading: Banking and Economy
